quiz.title: Unit 8 Quiz 12, Grade 10 Biology
quiz.subtitle: Immunology: Cell Types, Barriers, and Disease Classification
quiz.subject: Biology
quiz.topic: Cell-Mediated vs Humoral Immunity, Physical Barriers, First Line of Defense, Innate Immunity, Acquired Immunity, Non-Infectious Diseases

questions.question: Which of the following cells is involved in cell-mediated immunity?
questions.explanation: T-cells are involved in cell-mediated immunity, where they directly kill infected cells or activate other immune cells. B-cells are involved in humoral immunity (antibody production). Mast cells are involved in allergic responses.

questions.question: Which of the following vaccines produces the strongest immune memory?
questions.explanation: Live attenuated vaccines contain weakened live pathogens that replicate in the body, closely mimicking natural infection. This stimulates robust, long-lasting immune memory, often requiring only one or two doses. Inactivated, subunit, and toxoid vaccines are safer but typically require multiple doses and boosters for strong immunity.

questions.question: Which of the following does not act as a protecting barrier for the body surface?
questions.explanation: Salivary amylase is a digestive enzyme that breaks down starch, not a protective barrier. Skin (physical), mucus (mechanical), and gastric acid (chemical) all serve as protective barriers against pathogens.

questions.question: Which specific population segment gains the greatest protective advantage from the concept of community-wide immunity?
options: Physically active adults with robust internal defenses.
Individuals with compromised or weakened immune systems.
Professional athletes maintaining peak physical health.
International travelers visiting various diverse regions.
questions.explanation: Herd (community) immunity protects vulnerable populations who cannot be vaccinated or have weakened immune systems (infants, elderly, immunocompromised, chemotherapy patients). When a high percentage of the community is immune, pathogen transmission is reduced, indirectly protecting those who cannot develop strong immunity themselves.

questions.question: Select all that apply: Which of the following statements are TRUE?
options: Vaccines kill pathogens directly in the body
Herd immunity means most people are immune
Vaccination is a type of artificial passive immunity
Vaccines are most effective when given before exposure to a pathogen
Antigens in vaccines are recognized by red blood cells and neurons
questions.explanation: Herd immunity occurs when a high percentage of the population is immune, reducing transmission. Vaccines are most effective when given before exposure (prophylactic). Vaccines do NOT kill pathogens directly (they stimulate immunity), vaccination is artificial ACTIVE immunity (passive involves antibody transfer), and antigens are recognized by B and T cells, not red blood cells or neurons.

questions.question: Which disease has been eradicated through vaccination?
questions.explanation: Smallpox was declared eradicated in 1980 by the WHO after a global vaccination campaign. Polio has been eliminated in most countries but still exists in a few (not fully eradicated). Measles and tuberculosis are still present worldwide despite vaccination efforts.

questions.question: Which of the following cells is involved in humoral immunity?
questions.explanation: B-cells are involved in humoral immunity by producing antibodies that circulate in body fluids (humors). T-cells mediate cellular immunity, and mast cells are involved in allergic responses.

questions.question: Which of the following immunity is called the first line of defence?
questions.explanation: Innate immunity is the first line of defense, providing immediate, non-specific protection through physical, chemical, and cellular barriers. Active and passive immunity are types of acquired (adaptive) immunity.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a killed (inactivated) vaccine?
questions.explanation: Polio (IPV - Inactivated Polio Vaccine) is a killed vaccine containing inactivated poliovirus. Measles vaccine is a live attenuated vaccine, BCG (tuberculosis) is a live attenuated bacterial vaccine, and smallpox vaccine uses a live related virus (vaccinia).

questions.question: Booster doses are given to:
questions.explanation: Booster doses strengthen immunity by re-exposing the immune system to antigens, stimulating memory B and T cells to proliferate and maintain long-term protection. They do not replace failed vaccines, treat existing infections, or kill memory cells.

questions.question: Which immune molecule is produced in response to vaccination?
questions.explanation: Vaccination stimulates B cells to produce antibodies (immunoglobulins) against the antigen in the vaccine. Antigens are the molecules that trigger the immune response (not produced by the body), receptors are on cell surfaces, and aptamers are synthetic molecules not naturally produced by the immune system.

questions.question: Skin, body hair, cilia, eyelashes, the respiratory tract and the gastrointestinal tract are examples of ______.
questions.explanation: These are all examples of physical (mechanical) barriers that block pathogen entry. Cellular barriers involve phagocytes, cytokine barriers involve signaling molecules, and physiological barriers include temperature and pH.

questions.question: Which of the following is an example of a non-infectious disease?
options: Wheat rust caused by a fungus
Haemophilia caused by a gene mutation
questions.explanation: Haemophilia is a genetic disorder caused by gene mutations, making it non-infectious. Polio (viral), cholera (bacterial), and wheat rust (fungal) are all infectious diseases caused by pathogens.

questions.question: Which of the following is part of the innate immune response?
questions.explanation: Phagocytes (neutrophils, macrophages) are part of innate immunity. Antibodies and B lymphocytes are part of adaptive immunity. Stomach acid is a chemical barrier (innate) but not a cellular response.

questions.question: Which of the following biological defense mechanisms provides the most enduring protection against future pathogens by stimulating the production of memory cells?
questions.explanation: Active immunity (from infection or vaccination) stimulates memory cell production, providing long-lasting protection. Passive immunity (antibodies from mother or injection) is temporary, and innate barriers provide non-specific, immediate protection without memory.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered immunology concepts and disease classification! 🛡️
scoreMessages.70: Good job! Review the differences between cell-mediated and humoral immunity.
scoreMessages.50: Satisfactory. Focus on physical barriers and types of immunity.
scoreMessages.0: Please review T-cell vs B-cell functions, innate vs acquired immunity, and infectious vs non-infectious diseases.
